content: mr Durham's own room overlooked a large square filled with ancient trees, and was both well furnished and well lighted.
Into this Sir Simon and his nephew were ushered, and here they were greeted by the young lawyer.
Sir Simon had never spoken very well of Julius, but then he rarely said a good word of anyone.

"I am as well as can be expected," said Sir Simon, grumpily, taking his seat near the table, which was covered with books, and papers, and briefs, and red tape, and all the paraphernalia of legal affairs.
"About that will of mine-"
"Yes?" inquired Durham, sitting, with another glance at Beryl, and still more perplexed as to the baronet's motive for bringing the young man.
It is ready for signing."
"I can go, uncle, if you wish," said Julius, hastily, and rose.
"You are interested in the will."
"You wish me to read it all?" he asked hesitatingly.
Sir Simon nodded, and, leaning his chin on the knob of his cane, disposed himself to listen.
This was Julius, and he grew pale with pleasure as the lawyer proceeded.
The name of Bernard Gore was left out altogether.
Therefore he was annoyed that the latter should benefit to the exclusion of the former.
"Good!" said Sir Simon, who had followed the reading with close attention.
"Well?" he asked his nephew.
"We understand all that. Can you suggest any alteration?"
"No, uncle.
The will is perfect."
Sir Simon chuckled again.
"Yes, for disobedience.
"I know," said the young lawyer, nodding.
"She is the aunt of Lord Conniston, who told me about the matter."
"I thought Lord Conniston was in America," said Julius, sharply.
Do you know him?"
"Cove Castle," snapped Sir Simon.
But he never comes near the place.
Did you meet Lord Conniston at Miss Plantagenet's, Julius?"
"Aha!" chuckled Sir Simon.
"You did not fall in love with that girl?"
"No, uncle.
Of course I am engaged to Miss Randolph."
"no
I have not that pleasure."
"But no doubt Bernard has told you about her."
"I have not seen Gore for months."
"Are you sure?
"Yes!
I have charge of that money.
That was after he quarrelled with you, Sir Simon.
Since then I have not seen him."
"Then he does not know that I am in Crimea Square."
Certainly not from me.
It was Beryl who answered this.
"Bernard has enlisted as an Imperial Yeoman," said he.
"Every man who can, should go to the Front."
"If I had not my business to look after I certainly should," replied the lawyer.
"But regarding mr Gore.
"I can't say.
He deserves nothing.
"How good of you!"
Sir Simon chuckled yet again.
"I am quite of Julius's opinion," he declared.
"Bernard has behaved shamefully.
"They can live on ten thousand a year and be happy.
What is the use of getting more money than is needed?
If he does, he must take the consequences.
And he will-"
"Two hundred is quite enough," said the baronet.
"Can I not persuade you to-"
"No!
You can't persuade me to do anything but what I have done.
"Make your mind easy," said Sir Simon, dryly.
"I intend to live for many a year yet."
"I think I had better go now," observed Julius, rising.
"Won't you stop and see the will signed?"
I think it is better, as I inherit, that I should be out of the room.
Who knows but what Bernard might say, did I remain, that I exercised undue influence?"
"Uncle?"
"You can go if you like.
I shall see you on Friday when you come for Lucy."
"To take her to the Curtain Theatre.
Yes!
He chuckled to himself when he was outside, thinking of his good luck.
But at the door his face altered.
"He might change his mind," thought Beryl. "There's no reliance to be placed on him.
I wish-" he opened and shut his fist; "but he won't die for a long time."
Rather surprised, the lawyer did so, thinking the old man changeable.
He told me about Bernard being in love with that girl so as to create trouble."
Besides, people don't murder nowadays."
"Don't they?" said Sir Simon; "look in the newspapers."
I have led him on to think that the will has been made in his favor; and when he learns the truth he will be punished for his greed."
"By the way, you have the other will?"
"Yes!
It leaves everything to Bernard save the legacies, which remain much the same.
Of course, in the first will is mentioned an annuity to mrs Gilroy."
The fact is, I don't trust mrs Gilroy.
She's too friendly with Julius for my taste."
"Oh, she's on whatever side suits her," said Sir Simon, testily. "However, let the first will stand.
She's a poor thing and has had a hard life.
He found mrs Gilroy something of a mystery, and did not know what was the bond between her and Sir Simon.
"Never you mind.
"He thinks he has been disinherited?"
"Yes!
"No! not at present.
If we met, there would only be more trouble.
He has a temper inherited from his Italian mother, and I have a temper also.
But I don't want him to go to the war.
He must be bought out."
"I fear Bernard is not the man to be bought out."
All the same, I don't want him to be shot."
"There is only one way in which you can make him give up his soldiering."
"What's that?"
"He must keep away from me for a time.
After all, he is the son of his father, and, bad as Walter was, I loved him for his mother's sake.
As for the Italian woman-"
She is dead."
"I know she is.
But her brother Guiseppe is alive, and a scoundrel he is.
The other day he came to the Hall and tried to force his way into the house.
"What is his other name?"
"So she said.
She declared she was the member of a decayed Florentine family.
He is a musician, I believe-a plausible scamp.
"I know that," snapped the old man.
"You don't know.
He is young after all.
But you know, by the will, I have put it out of Bernard's power to assist Tolomeo.
"Well, I was at Eton with him, you know, and we are great friends.
"Leave matters as they are.
Then we'll see.
But I think a little hardship will do him good. He behaved very badly."
"Bernard is naturally hot tempered."
"So am i Therefore, let us keep apart for a time.
Who knows what would happen did we meet.
I shan't interfere with her romance."
"The Red Window.
What's that?"
I have no time to repeat rubbish.
Meanwhile, should Bernard be hard up, help him out of your own pocket.
She gave a gasp.
"I wonder if he died hard.
There was no doubt that life was extinct, although it could not have been so long.
The skin was still warm to the touch, but that might have been because the room was heated. Also, the dead man was seated close to the fire.
"How terrible!" muttered Bernard, whose emotions were not yet under control.
"I must get help."
He turned to go, but the housekeeper, suddenly becoming endowed with life, flung herself in his path.
"No!" she said harshly.
"Don't seek help if you value your life."
"The servants are up-a policeman is evidently coming along.
I must-"
It is known that your grandfather disinherited you, and-"
"You know I did not commit this crime."
"I know nothing.
"You killed him, you strangled him.
"Let me go," cried Bernard, thinking he had been drawn into a trap.
But mrs Gilroy still clung with a force for which he would not have given her credit.
Shrieking aloud she was dragged by the startled young man into the passage and towards the landing.
He will escape!"
"Yes, yes! for murder-murder!" cried mrs Gilroy, breathlessly.
This is his grandson.
"I did not enter-"
"Anything you say now will be used in evidence against you," said the policeman.
"Come up the stairs, we must see this corpse.
"I came here-"
"To kill Sir Simon.
Jane," cried mrs Gilroy, turning to the housemaid. "Don't you see who it is?"
Jane staggered forward supported by the cook.
Whatever did you-"
"I thought so," cried mrs Gilroy, triumphantly.
"Cook."
"I know him too," said the cook, keeping well out of the way.
"Yes! but-"
"Then you are guilty."
"There is some mistake."
"The magistrate will decide that.
"You came to see Sir Simon after ten.
I let you in myself.
I waited below while you spoke with Sir Simon, and you left fifteen minutes ago.
I went upstairs to see my master.
He was dead-strangled.
"I had only just come."
"Come back, you mean," said the officer.
"Oh, Bernard-Bernard!" sobbed Jane, "how could you kill him!
Lor'! that I should have kissed a murderer."
"Hark!" said mrs Gilroy, raising her hand, "footsteps.
The other police are coming.
"This is a trick-a trap!" cried Bernard, struggling to get free.
"I never was in the house before-"
"You have visited in the kitchen for over a fortnight," said Jane, weeping copiously.
Look well, girl.
Am I the man?"
"Bring him under the lamp, policeman," said Jane, hesitating.
"No!" said mrs Gilroy, pushing the housemaid back, "there is no time. Here are the police.
We must go upstairs and see Sir Simon.
Miss Randolph is at the theatre with mr Beryl.
There was a sound of approaching wheels, and a moment afterwards a carriage drove up.
Out of it stepped Lucy and Julius.
"What's this?" asked Julius.
"Bernard," cried Lucy, running forward, "what have you done?"
"Murdered his grandfather, miss," said the policeman.
"Keep him fast, sir," she said savagely.
